questions.question: You are creating a business case and a project charter with a small initial project team. You have a high-level feature decomposed into smaller release milestones and you'd like to note a high-level schedule using wideband Delphi or a high-level version of planning poker. What are you going to do next?
options: A. Hold a round of cards with points on them where each team member places their cards in the center for the total estimate
B. Ask subject matter experts to complete multiple rounds of estimates individually, compare and explain the highest and lowest estimates for each round
C. Show the wideband high-level range from the lowest to highest for each feature
D. Find the business functionality for each feature and estimate the effort based on that
questions.explanation: The wideband Delphi technique asks subject matter experts to complete multiple rounds of estimates individually and compare and explain the highest and lowest estimates for each round. Planning poker is a variation of wideband Delphi. Relative estimating with a specific range shown (option C) is not the correct process for wideband Delphi.

questions.question: You have been asked to help facilitate a senior planning meeting in your organization. High-level executives have made a presentation on the strengths and opportunities of the organization, painting a rosy picture. However, revenue has fallen recently and the chief executive asks you to level out their analysis with a more complete view. What will you do next?
options: A. Add your organization's competitors and compare their products
B. Showcase the pipeline of upcoming work to be delivered in the next quarter
C. Add the background and abilities of the executive team that will allow the organization to deliver
D. Brainstorm and capture the weaknesses of the organization and threats to the organization
questions.explanation: This question refers to SWOT analysis (Strengths, Weaknesses, Opportunities, Threats). Once strengths and opportunities have been captured, a complete picture also involves the weaknesses and threats to provide a balanced view. The other options do not complete the SWOT framework analysis.

questions.question: You are adding an essential feature to your product roadmap. Your team believes they can complete the feature in 6 weeks. However, the feature is quite large and a bit more complicated than normal. One of your lead developers says they worked on a similar feature in another company and it took around 4 months to complete. What will you do next?
options: A. Use relative estimating where the smallest piece of work is assigned as one and everything else is relative to that
B. Use analogous estimating and place the feature with around 4 months to complete so you are a little bit safer
C. Use parametric estimating to identify parameters such as cost or hours to estimate the feature
D. Use function point estimating to show the amount of business functionality in the feature
questions.explanation: Analogous estimating is appropriate when there are people who have experience delivering a similar item. The lead developer's past experience with a similar feature in another company provides a good basis for analogous estimating, which allows you to estimate effort quickly and with medium-level accuracy. The 4-month estimate provides a valuable reference point compared to the team's 6-week estimate.

questions.question: You have a prioritized backlog of features. You have decomposed the next feature to be delivered into user stories for a 2-week sprint. The team would like to estimate the effort involved on those user stories but are unsure how to proceed. What will you do next?
options: A. Ask each team member to add their own estimates as they complete the acceptance criteria
B. Estimate the effort for the overall feature and reduce the need for individual story estimates
C. Help the team perform relative estimating where the stories are sized in points relative to the smallest piece
D. Base the estimates on cost per hour and add them after the work is done to ensure accuracy
questions.explanation: Relative estimating is the most common method for user stories where they are sized in comparison to the smallest piece, often in story points and frequently on a Fibonacci scale. This method benefits from input from all team members (testers, developers, etc.) and provides velocity insights for sprint planning. Individual estimates (option A) lack the collaborative benefits, and basing estimates on actual hours after completion (option D) is not estimation—it's just recording what happened.
questions.topic: Estimating Techniques - Relative Estimating for User Stories

questions.question: You are halfway through your current 2-week sprint. The work is progressing well. However, you notice that there is not enough work ready and elaborated for when the next sprint begins. What will you do next?
options: A. Hold a backlog refinement meeting and work with the team to identify the work that could be accomplished
B. Raise the issue as a blocker in the daily standup and ask your team to swarm around the problem
C. Raise the issue in the next retrospective and take an action for the team
D. Place the cards on the kanban board so the team can see the work and get started
questions.explanation: At a backlog refinement meeting, the backlog is progressively elaborated and prioritized to identify the work that can be accomplished in an upcoming iteration. Since you are halfway through the current sprint, a backlog refinement meeting is the appropriate action to prepare work for the next sprint. A retrospective (option C) happens at the end of a sprint and would be too late, and swarming (option B) takes people off current work rather than preparing the work properly.
